subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Analysis of $f$ from Graph of $g = f'$
stimulus:
The graph of the continuous function $g$, the derivative of the function $f$, is shown above. The function $g$ is piecewise linear for $-5 \leq x < 3$, and $g(x) = 2(x - 4)^2$ for $3 \leq x \leq 6$.

From the graph: $g(-5) = 0$, $g(-3) = 2$, $g(0) = -2$, $g(1) = 0$, $g(3) = 2$. The piecewise linear portions connect these points with straight line segments. Note that $f'(x) = g(x)$.

parts.prompt: If $f(1) = 3$, what is the value of $f(-5)$?

scoring_points.description: Correctly uses the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: $f(-5) = f(1) - \int_{-5}^{1} g(x)\,dx$. Computes $\int_{-5}^{1} g(x)\,dx$ as the net signed area under $g$ from $-5$ to $1$ using the triangular regions from the graph: triangle on $[-5,-3]$ above the $x$-axis has area $\frac{1}{2}(2)(2) = 2$; triangle on $[-3,0]$ below the $x$-axis has area $-\frac{1}{2}(3)(2) = -3$; triangle on $[0,1]$ below the $x$-axis has area $-\frac{1}{2}(1)(2) = -1$. Net area $= 2 - 3 - 1 = -2$.

required_elements: $\int_{-5}^{1} g(x)\,dx = -2$ (correct net signed area from graph)

scoring_points.description: Sets up the integral using the formula $g(x) = 2(x-4)^2$ on $[3, 6]$: $\int_3^6 2(x-4)^2\,dx$

parts.prompt: For $-5 < x < 6$, on what open intervals, if any, is the graph of $f$ both increasing and concave up? Give a reason for your answer.

scoring_points.description: Correctly states the conditions: $f$ is increasing when $f'(x) = g(x) > 0$, and $f$ is concave up when $f''(x) = g'(x) > 0$ (i.e., when $g$ is increasing). From the graph: $g > 0$ on $(-5, -3)$ and on $(1, 6)$. $g$ is increasing on $(-5, -3)$ (slope of the linear piece is positive) and on $(0, 4)$ (slope of linear piece from $0$ to $1$ is positive, then $g'(x) = 4(x-4) < 0$ for $3 < x < 4$ on the parabola — actually $g$ is increasing on $(0,1)$ and on $(4,6)$). The intervals where both conditions hold are where $g > 0$ AND $g$ is increasing.

required_elements: $f$ is increasing when $g(x) > 0$; $f$ is concave up when $g'(x) > 0$ (i.e., $g$ is increasing)

scoring_points.description: Correct intervals with reason: $(-5, -3)$ and $(4, 6)$. On $(-5,-3)$: $g(x) > 0$ (so $f$ is increasing) and $g$ is increasing (so $f$ is concave up). On $(4,6)$: $g(x) = 2(x-4)^2 > 0$ and $g'(x) = 4(x-4) > 0$ (so $f$ is concave up).

required_elements: Intervals $(-5, -3)$ and $(4, 6)$; $g > 0$ and $g$ is increasing on each interval

parts.prompt: Find the $x$-coordinate of each point of inflection of the graph of $f$. Give a reason for your answer.

scoring_points.description: Correctly identifies that inflection points of $f$ occur where $f''(x) = g'(x)$ changes sign, i.e., where $g$ changes from increasing to decreasing or from decreasing to increasing. From the graph: $g$ changes from increasing to decreasing at $x = -3$; $g$ changes from decreasing to increasing at $x = 0$; $g(x) = 2(x-4)^2$ on $[3,6]$ has $g'(x) = 4(x-4)$, which changes sign from negative to positive at $x = 4$.

required_elements: Inflection points of $f$ occur where $g$ changes monotonicity (where $g'$ changes sign)

scoring_points.description: Correct $x$-coordinates of inflection points: $x = -3$, $x = 0$, and $x = 4$. At each of these, $g'(x) = f''(x)$ changes sign, so the concavity of $f$ changes.
